I know I said I'd start with the TODO/REVIEW items, but I ended up reading everything anyway because I had to find those items.

For the most part, I'm very pleased. There are some fiddly bits with comments inline (e.g. pyjwt).
The only broad/overarching thing that I think we need to address is `ToStr`. I have some inline comments on it, but, in short, I think `object` and most (all?) primitive types satisfy `ToStr`, which makes it very similar to `Any`. e.g.
```python
>>> import typing as t
>>> class ToStr(t.Protocol):
...     def __str__(self) -> str: ...
...
>>> rt = t.runtime_checkable(ToStr)
>>> x = object()
>>> isinstance(x, rt)
True
>>> def foo(): return 1
...
>>> isinstance(foo, rt)
True
>>> y = (1, 1)
>>> isinstance(y, rt)
True
```

So I think we may need to rethink that approach. For some cases, `Any` may actually be appropriate. And in others, we may want to define a smaller `Union` type, like `ID_PARAM_TYPE`.

Great, Thank you @tomassosorio for the catch and providing the clear description of the problem!

Overall, this looks really good. I have a lot of small comments. On a larger level, two things stand out:

1. There's a few places where comments or docs look like they're not 100% consistent with the implementation.
2. The logic makes sense, but the method names aren't very meaningful, and that makes it harder to follow. Could we make them more expressive of what's actually happening?

Thanks very much for working on this @mayer79 !

First, I think that before exporting these callbacks, we should change the names. I don't think R packages should use names with dots for anything other than S3 methods. There are many things in this package that violate that rule (e.g. `lgb.train()`, `lgb.cv()`) and they've been left unchanged for historical reasons, but I'd like to take this opportunity where new functions are being exported to try to avoid introducing more such names.

What do you think about this?

* `cb.early.stop()` --> `cb_early_stop()`
* `cb.print.evaluation()` --> `cb_print_evaluation()`
* `cb.record.evaluation()` --> `cb_record_evaluation()`

If you agree with that, would you be willing to open a separate PR just changing those names?

<hr>

> passing a list of callback functions to `lgb.train()` etc. does not yet work properly because the "old" parameters `early_stopping_rounds`, `eval_freq` etc. override the callback list. How should we deal with this?

Thanks for pointing this out! Over in the Python package, we've recently removed these special keyword arguments to `lgb.train()` and `lgb.cv()` in favor of encouraging the use of callbacks (see #4574 and #4908).

I think the R package should do something similar. That would mean:

1. For now, raise deprecation warnings in `lgb.train()` and `lgb.cv()` if keyword argument `early_stopping_rounds`, `eval_freq`, etc. are non-NULL, and in those deprecation warnings encourage the use of the corresponding callbacks.
2. Add an issue to the backlog to remove those warnings and arguments after the release of LightGBM 4.0.

HOWEVER...we also should continue to support the case where parameters like `early_stopping_rounds` is passed via `params`.

In the Python package, the possibility of passing both `params={"early_stopping_rounds": 10}` and `callbacks=[lgb.early_stopping(15)]` isn't handled explicitly. If the parameter `early_stopping_rounds` is passed in `params`, an `lgb.early_stopping()` is added to the `callbacks` list.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/f185695617d29e446e021fed1261ba84f09ff992/python-package/lightgbm/engine.py#L198-L209

I think that what will happen in those places is that both callbacks will be evaluated on each iteration, and early stopping will happen if either of them thinks early stopping has been met (I'd have to test that to be sure).

I think that in the R package, since we're just newly exporting the callbacks, we should be stricter and raise an error prior to training beginning if `early_stopping_rounds` from `params` and `cb_early_stopping()` are both provided.

The name and all arguments passed in are stored in the callback function's attributes...

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/f185695617d29e446e021fed1261ba84f09ff992/R-package/R/callback.R#L318-L319

...so it should be possible to check whether or not any of the functions in `callbacks` have name `"cb.early.stop"` AND whether the values passed to that function conflict with `early_stopping_rounds` passed through `params`.

I think we're making some assumptions in this diff:
- GetLogdirSubdirectories is always called (e.g. by plugin_event_accumulator) before ListDirectoryAbsolute is called on the lower level dir (e.g. by directory_watcher). Because the cache build by the former will be used by that latter
- GetLogdirSubdirectories is called on the highest top level dir once, because we'll clear cache every time
- Our implementation of listdir_bfs will be just as or more efficient than gfile.walk, since we're now implementing walk explicitly instead of relying on the walk implementation of each filesystem
- Without a TTL for the cache, we're no longer supporting watching of new files as they're created after TB initial load

I think we need much fewer change, if we simply build a look aside cache with TTL inside ListDirectoryAbsolute, without touching GetLogdirSubdirectories?

Would love to hear thoughts from TB team.

@abhi-glitchhg Thanks a lot for your work on this.


I've run similar checks on my side. On your first script that confirms that we produce the same results, I replaced the `assert_close` with `equal` to ensure it matches exactly and it does.

For benchmarks, I modified slightly your script to move the random input generation out of the loops and revert the stack that was part of the previous solution. Here is the updated script:
```python
import time
import torch
from torchvision.models.detection._utils import BoxLinearCoder

device = "cpu"
boxes = [torch.randn(3, 4).to(device=device) for i in range(5)]
rel_codes = torch.randn(5, 3, 4).to(device=device)

box_coder = BoxLinearCoder(True)

# Warmup

for _ in range(1000):
    original = [
            box_coder.decode_single(bbox_regression_per_image, anchors_per_image)
            for anchors_per_image, bbox_regression_per_image in zip(boxes, rel_codes)
        ]
    original = torch.stack(original)
    new = box_coder.decode_all(rel_codes, boxes)


start = time.process_time()
for _ in range(10000):
    original = [
            box_coder.decode_single(bbox_regression_per_image, anchors_per_image)
            for anchors_per_image, bbox_regression_per_image in zip(boxes, rel_codes)
        ]
    original = torch.stack(original)
print(f"time taken for decode_single: {time.process_time() -start}")

start = time.process_time()
for _ in range(10000):
    new = box_coder.decode_all(rel_codes, boxes)

print(f"time taken for decode_all: {time.process_time()-start}")

print('done ')
```

On CPU I get a 5.5x improvement and on GPU about 5x. So LGTM!

Please push back if I'm not looking at this the right way, but I am not fully convinced on the value of the guide in its current form.

From the title and first sentence, I was expecting that this guide would be a sequence of items in the form "if you are trying to do X, but encounter problem Y, you should do Z".  E.g. "I ran a schedule to update my asset every hour, but noticed my asset isn't getting updated, so I should look in these places for errors"?

My read of the current structure is that it's kind of a survey of the different places in Dagit that errors can pop up. I believe that most users will discover those naturally when they use the product.

So my instinct would be to do one of the following:
- Change the content to be in the form of "if you are trying to do X, but encounter problem Y, you should do Z".
- Punt on this until we have a better picture of the important Xs, Ys, and Zs to cover. I think that writing a useful troubleshooting guide like this can be really tough, because it requires building an understanding of the the top Xs, Ys, and Zs.

I realize that I'm coming to this late in the game, so please talk me down from this if you think I'm looking at it the wrong way or missing important context.

i don't think this type of logging should happen in the methods that return the values, regardless of whether they're cached. i understand it's convenient, but IMO logging of config variables should go into startup routines.
since this is specific to lambda, and will only be relevant if lambda is started, i suggest putting it in the `start_lambda` function.

and maybe we can allow ourselves to generalize a bit:
i suggest introducing a `lambda:` namespace in the even names (just a convention), and logging a `lambda:config` event. which would transform the logging line into something like:

```python
log.event("lambda:config", version="v1", default_executor_mode=default_executor_mode)
```
that event can then be a generic container for additional config variables of lambda we may want to track in the future.

the version discriminator helps us separate the config for the new v2 provider.

I think there's probably some more detail to go into here, but my skim of the extraction AST parsing code looks reasonable to me.

The main blocker is that we have to generate JSON files on a per frontend bundle basis, not per plugin. This is because different Javascript assets may be injected into different SPAs (for example the side nav bundles are used on every SPA and need strings).

To avoid having to load all the strings for every plugin on every page, we should reinstate the per-bundle JSON generation. We can maintain per plugin CSV generation for translator convenience.

So, we can use our plugin parsing machinery that we use for code building, and use this to also generate the file paths we search in for string extraction.

In the process of this, we can also extract the specific webpack configurations for each plugin (which will allow us to properly traverse aliased import paths). We can then also use the webpack configuration to discover the entry points of each of our frontend bundles, and extract the appropriate strings for each of those into the relevant bundle JSON from our translated CSVs.

This means that we still get to upload strings from unimported files, because we are extracting strings via globbing, but we are only loading used strings at runtime. Further, it means that if someone has been a bit naughty and has imported strings from another plugin, then we can allow at JSON generation time for that string to be duplicated into the JSON files for multiple frontend bundles, meaning we won't get caught out by cross component translation issues.

I think the parts of this PR which put in testing for result codes and raising an exception are good.

I don't like the parts which take an exception replace it with a different exception, like this:
```
        except Exception as e:
            raise SubmitException(job_name,
                                  f'Submission of command failed due to {e}')
```

This style means that someone trying to debug an error later on loses access to stack trace information about what happened further inside the code.

Maybe it would be good to make SubmitException able to store the entire exception which it is hiding in that case.

Or maybe, SubmitException shouldn't happen here: we already know that something is wrong because an exception is already being raised, so there is no need to raise a different exception.

Nice work! I left some minor comments.

**Design question:** At the API level, could we consolidate `prefetch_blocks` and `async_fetch_batch` with a single `prefetch_batches` argument that gives the number of batches to asynchronously prefetch?

## Motivation

I think this will be a lot cleaner from an end-user perspective, with a single argument controlling prefetching behavior that has clear batch-based semantics. We ultimately care about **pipelining upstream batch preparation with downstream batch computation**, and both prefetching batches over the network and background batch slicing/concatenation/formatting fall under the former, so having a single batch-based argument controlling how many batches we want to prepare while a single batch is being processed seems like better UX. IMO, the object store transfer prefetching should probably be an implementation detail in facilitating a generic "how many batches to we want to prepare in the background on this worker" API, similar to the semantics of the [`tf.data` prefetching API](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/data/Dataset#prefetch).

## Do We Need Two Knobs?

Since this seems like nicer UX, the only reasons that we'd want to have two knobs (that I can think of) are:
1. We want to have different levels of prefetching for object store transfers compared to background slicing/concatenation/format conversion.
2. The prefetching unit for the two args should be different (e.g. batch vs. block).

(1) could be true. If we assume that we're not hitting NIC bandwidth or CPU bottlenecks during deserialization or batch prep, then prefetching object store transfers is ultimately limited by the node's object store capacity, while background slicing/concatenation/format conversion is ultimately limited by the node's worker heap, which are separate resource pools with different capacities. This is further complicated by format conversions leading to memory inflation. E.g. for a Pandas batch format UDF on Arrow blocks executed on a node that has equal object store and worker heap allocations, you could imagine that we want to prefetch 8 batches to the object store, but only do 1 background batch slice and format conversion since the Pandas format conversion results in a DataFrame that's 4x larger than the Arrow data (and the UDF will need to at least allocate an output in the worker heap as well); this inflation could easily happen for boolean data, for example, which is bitpacked in Arrow but is not in Pandas.

However, this may be a non-issue if:
* the Python worker heap is typically the bottleneck rather than the node's object store (I think this is true for transformations/trainers that don't use a NumPy/Arrow narrow waist), or;
* we can rely on Ray Core's object pull manager to do proper backpressure on object transfers (I believe this is true).

The user can set `prefetch_batches` tailored to whatever eliminates compute resource stalls subject to a Python worker heap cap, and object transfer prefetching will do a best-effort fulfillment of `prefetch_batches`, subject to Ray Core's backpressure on object transfers when the node's object store pressure gets high. I'm thinking that this might make (1) a non-issue.

(2) is the status quo, but I think that the current object store prefetching API would be better expressed in terms of batches (this has been a long-standing TODO), so I don't think that (2) is a concern.

## Semantics and Implementation

For `.map_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics, except that the buffer size is configurable. Down the line, we might have this arg control the number of blocks that are prefetched by actor workers under the actor compute strategy, rather than that being a strategy config.

For `.iter_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics along with exposing the buffer size, _and_ would control how many blocks are prefetched into the object store on the consumer/worker node. Namely, if we know the number of rows in a block from the readily-available block metadata (this should be defined in the common case), we would prefetch `N` blocks such that `N` is the smallest number of blocks that satisfies `prefetch_batches`, i.e. we choose an `N` such that `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N]) >= prefetch_batches * batch_size` and `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N-1]) < prefetch_batches * batch_size`. If the block metadata doesn't have the number of rows in the block (e.g. the dataset is a lazy read of CSV files, for which we don't know the number of rows until the entire files has been read), then we can prefetch one block at a time.

@ericl @jianoaix In general, consistently resetting the index was a very early design decision, where we decided that a block's index should represent the dataset record order; this means that we need to reset the index whenever we slice, concatenate, or shuffle blocks, with the end-result being that each block is locally ordered, and if all blocks are concatenated together (e.g. via `ds.to_pandas()`), the resulting index would give the global dataset order. This was under the assumption that the user would _not_ be encoding application-level identifiers into the index, since we hadn't encountered that use case at the time.

@jianoaix This particular index drop was added because of this issue: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/27019/commits/a48e34d25477f4d0f90b7bb7ac4909c8a042b696#r931308133.

If we're going to try to maintain the index, we can't only make the change on concatenation; we would need to do a global audit of all other places we reset the index, e.g.:
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L147
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L154
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L167

And before we decide to try to maintain the index, we need to ensure that index-aware operations (i.e. operations that automatically align on indices) will not give incorrect results, and we might want to only preserve the original index if the index is not the default sequential ordering index (assuming there's an easy way to check that).

@ArturNiederfahrenhorst I just did a more thorough pass on your LSTM tests. The test is not really testing the rollout of LSTM cells during training (test_forward_train)

What you are doing right now:
- stacking obs, actions, and states in multiple BATCHES. Where is the time dimension then?
- This is like using LSTM cells as a fully connected cell :) 

What you should be doing:
- stack obs, and actions in both batches and times. So that the final shape of batch is (bxt, ho) and the final shape of actions is (bxt, ha)
- stack the state_in to be of shape (b, n_layer=1, hs) 
- if the number of steps are not compatible with (b, t) reshaping you need to zero pad. For example if you took 30 steps and your max_rollout_len = 20. You need to create a (1, 20, ho) obs tensor and concat if with a (1, 10, ho) obs. You cannot. So you need to zero pad the second one to make it (1, 20, ho) and take care of zeros with masking. the obs shape in this case should be come (2x20, ho) with bunch of zeros in there. In this example your state_in shape should be (2, 1, hs).

Thanks a lot @jrbourbeau for a quick fix of this regression.

I'm eager to help fix the problem. At the same time, I want to understand this bug a bit better in order to avoid creating additional technical debt within zarr-python.

When I first saw @djhoese's issue, I was puzzled by the recommend fix. The example was using local file storage. How is it possible that creating arrays in local files is completely broken in zarr? Surely our test suite would have caught such a regression.

I dug deeper and realized that dask.array.to_zarr is always converting string arguments to FSMap objects (see https://github.com/dask/dask/blob/40bc376acb1f631aa80db17ce4fb779e1a888aee/dask/array/core.py#L3677-L3681) using code like

```python
    if isinstance(url, str):
        mapper = get_mapper(url, **storage_options)
```

FSMap has been effectively deprecated by the introduction of zarr.storage.FSStore (beginning with #546). FSStore has additional performance optimization that are not available with FSMap, and therefore we always prefer to use an FSStore (although an FSMap is still a valid store, as it is a valid MutableMapping).

To address this inconsistency, #1304 recently implemented a change to automatically promote an FSMap to an FSStore (thanks @ravwojdyla! 🙌 ). That change is what revealed this latent bug. Before, the FSMap was initialized with whatever mode the user chose (or the default from fsspec, presumably  `mode='w'`). Now we override this with the default keyword argument from `normalize_store_arg` which is indeed `mode='r'`:

https://github.com/zarr-developers/zarr-python/blob/4e633ad9aa434304296900790c4c65e0fa0dfa12/zarr/storage.py#L144-L147

`mode` is not an attribute of an FSMap, but it is an attribute of FSStore. It is used internally within Zarr to implement read-only behavior, e.g.

https://github.com/zarr-developers/zarr-python/blob/4e633ad9aa434304296900790c4c65e0fa0dfa12/zarr/storage.py#L1409-L1411

My concern is that the `mode` argument has a somewhat ambiguous status within Zarr. It is not supported by all stores, but it could be. That inconsistency is what opened the possibility for this bug in the first place.

Having written all that, I think I have convinced myself that I am fine with this fix. 🙃 

The two follow-up actions I would suggest are:
- Do an audit on the status of `mode` in Zarr. All stores could potentially support mode in the same way that FSStore does. However, we also support a different, redundant method of specifying read-only: via the `Array` and `Group` constructors. `DirectoryStore`, for example, doesn't have `mode` at all, but it is still possible to use read-only stores on disk. Check out [`open_array`](https://github.com/zarr-developers/zarr-python/blob/main/zarr/creation.py#L397) for more details.
- Consider refactoring `dask.array.to_zarr`. Now that Zarr implements url parsing internally via fsspec, it should be possible to leave the store creation completely up to Zarr, rather than Dask.

Instead of having the `copy_transformer_vocab` function, I think it would be better to just have a method on the `Vocabulary` class like this:

```python
class Vocabulary:
    # ...
    def add_transformer_vocab(self, model_name: str, namespace: str) -> None:
        # ... basically the same logic that's in `copy_transformer_vocab`

Then the `PretrainedTransformerIndexer` can just call this method. And `Vocabulary.from_pretrained_transformer` could just look like this:

```python
@classmethod
def from_pretrained_transformer(cls, model_name: str, namespace: str = "tokens") -> "Vocabulary":
    vocab = cls.empty()
    vocab.add_transformer_vocab(model_name, namespace)
    return vocab
```

I like the updates. A few questions/comments arise from the new Shim objects:
1.  Do you envision that anyone would use a `Shim` object directly, or will they always use a `GetAttrShim` or some other derived class?  Can you think of any other potential derived class from `Shim`?  I'm essentially wondering if `Shim` is an abstract base class or not.
2.  I like the use of the `_assign_to_dill_module` decorator. I'm not in love with the name.  I have no suggestions for an alternate at the moment. Do you think it's possible that a shim might be assigned to a module that is not `dill._dill`, for example like `dill.source`?
3.  The interface for `__init__` is not clear for any of the `Shim` objects.  What type of object is `reduction`?  Upon code inspection, it seems that it's a 2-tuple. Is that necessarily the case? What format is expected?  I assume the format of a reduction function. That should be obvious (i.e documented). Similarly, what type of object is `object`, `name`, and `default`?  I recognize that the intent is that the names and types are the same as for `getattr`, but that's also potentially not obvious. For example, does a `GetAttrShim` behave completely like `getattr` with regard to `default`? At the very least, `__init__` needs user docs or inline docs describing the expected/required interface for each of the `Shim` classes.

I'm probably over-engineering things, but I'm slightly concerned about the circular references that get created. Once you create an AggregateSensor, it can't be garbage collected until its target dies (and even then, only with the circular garbage detector). For katgpucbf that's probably not an issue, but I'm a bit concerned about katsdpcontroller which tends to dynamically create and destroy sensors.

To fix that, I think you'd need to:
- Add a ` __del__` that deregisters the callbacks from the sensors and target.
- Use callbacks that bind a weakref to the AggregateSensor (possibly static methods with functools.partial, although maybe WeakProxy would work).

Just took a read through, please correct my understanding if I'm mistaken!
- So basically this is running a trickle timer and sending announces (by starting an 'announce cycle') when it expires.
- Fixed trickle 'interval' (I) (Currently 11 min 28 seconds) (so we don't use the adaptive interval of trickle)
- Redundancy constant of 8 (k) ( so if we receive 8 announces in the 'window', we don't bother starting an 'announce cycle')
- The actual 'window' for trickle is randomly between interval/2 and interval
- So we would expect an absolute maximum of 16 announces per channel in a 5 min 44 second interval.

Concern: When a trickle timer event is 'triggered', this triggers an 'announce cycle' over a period of time that is very likely to exceed the trickle timer window length. It seems like the mechanisms within trickle work best if the 'transmission time' as RFC6206 would describe it is much less than the interval. Otherwise the delay between action and feedback are going to cause oscillations in the number of announces sent on the network, and the announces will likely exceed the above limit.

It looks like RFC6202 has some recommendations on how to set the values, such as sections 5 and 6.6. I think just cycling through every channel on a channel page (with say half-second delay and half-second jitter) would be more suitable than staggering it across an entire interval period. This would allow trickle to function properly, and still shouldn't cause any disruption on the node.

A general good practice -

Such pre-commit changes (`pyupgrade` can be added as a pre-commit) should be carried out in a single commit (that is - changing "some pyupgrade suggestions" to "all pyupgrade suggestions"). 

This commit's hash should then be added in the `.git-blame-ignore-revs` file (does not exist in Zarr, but should be added in the root folder) to preserve `git blame`.

There are a couple changes in the PR that I have suggested. These are optional and do not affect the functionality of the code. Overall, I approve the merging of this pull request ✅ .

But, I am also adding the following so we know which direction to go for future improvements.

### Error handling: still not ideal

There is a still one thing about our error handling that bothers me. I can illustrate it with the following error message which was generated by raising a plain exception in the function body:

```
# >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> ERROR REPORT <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1787, in parse_known_args
        namespace, args = self._parse_known_args(args, namespace)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1996, in _parse_known_args
        stop_index = consume_positionals(start_index)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1952, in consume_positionals
        take_action(action, args)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1845, in take_action
        argument_values = self._get_values(action, argument_strings)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 2386, in _get_values
        self._check_value(action, value[0])
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 2433, in _check_value
        raise ArgumentError(action, msg % args)
    argparse.ArgumentError: argument command: invalid choice: 'string-art' (choose from 'clean', 'compare', 'config', 'create', 'info', 'init', 'install', 'list', 'package', 'remove', 'rename', 'run', 'search', 'uninstall', 'update', 'upgrade', 'notices')

    During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/exceptions.py", line 1133, in __call__
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/cli/main.py", line 63, in main_subshell
        args = p.parse_args(args)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1755, in parse_args
        args, argv = self.parse_known_args(args, namespace)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1794, in parse_known_args
        self.error(str(err))
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/cli/conda_argparse.py", line 197, in error
        sys.exit(subcommand.action(sys.argv[2:]))
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/string-art/string_art.py", line 26, in sub_command
        raise Exception("Hai")
    Exception: Hai
```

We're now bubbling up an exception, which is good, but because of the way that we use `argparse` we also get a weird looking exception from that code as well. This admittedly isn't that bad, but it could really confuse developers working on new plugins. They may choose to resolve the first error, only to later discover that this exception is in fact part of the functioning code.

### Potential improvements

During the first week of August as I was playing around with the new plugins code (this pull request), I really wanted to tackle this problem myself and actually wrote up an alternative implementation that addresses these issues:

https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11680

This implementation also enables support for CLI argument parsing libraries like `click` (see the example in the PR).

For future iterations, we should try to integrate some of the changes I suggested there.

Consider the scenario - 
User creates a TKG-plus cluster with CSE 3.0 (api_version 35, enable_tkg_plus: true)
User upgrades CSE to CSE 3.1 (legacy_mode: false, enable_tkg_plus: false)

Now, if we process all the TKG-plus clusters to have RDE 2.0, when cluster list is issued, TKG-plus cluster will be shown even when CSE server is running with the configuration enable_tkg_plus: false.

I think we should fail the upgrade (as we do in other cases) if we encounter a TKG+ cluster.
